assessment_items.question_content:
Justin's dad claims that taking piano lessons will help Justin get into an Ivy League school one day. Justin decides to try to prove his dad wrong by interviewing a randomly selected sample of college-educated adults. Justin asks them whether they attended an Ivy League school and also whether they took piano lessons as a child. 

The relative frequency table below shows his results. Justin interviewed $9$ randomly selected adults who attended an Ivy League school but did not take piano lessons.  

|  | Piano lessons| No piano lessons| Row total
- | - | -
**Ivy League** | $\begin{align} &\text{Row %} \\ &\text{Column %} \\ &\text{Total %} \end{align}$  | $\begin{align} &\text{25} \\ &\text{8.3} \\ &\text{2.1} \end{align}$ | $\begin{align} &\text{75} \\ &\text{8.3} \\ &\text{6.25} \end{align}$ | $\begin{align} &\text{100} \\ &\text{------} \\ &\text{8.3} \end{align}$
**Not Ivy League ** | $\begin{align} &\text{Row %} \\ &\text{Column %} \\ &\text{Total %} \end{align}$ |$\begin{align} &\text{25} \\ &\text{91.7} \\ &\text{22.9} \end{align}$  | $\begin{align} &\text{75} \\ &\text{91.7} \\ &\text{$68.75$} \end{align}$| $\begin{align} &\text{100} \\ &\text{-----} \\ &\text{91.7} \end{align}$
**Column total** | $\begin{align} & \text{Row %}\\&\text{Column %}\\&\text{Total %}\end{align}$ | $\begin{align} &\text{-----} \\ &\text{100} \\ &\text{25} \end{align}$  | $\begin{align} &\text{-----} \\ &\text{100} \\ &\text{75} \end{align}$| $\begin{align} &\text{100} \\ &\text{100} \\ &\text{100} \end{align}$
  
**How many adults in Justin's survey took piano lessons?**  
(Round to the nearest whole number.)

**Does the table show evidence of an association between not taking piano lessons and not attending an Ivy League school?**  
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:

The pink $6.25$ in the table below shows us that $6.25\%$ of all the people in the survey attended an Ivy League school but did not take piano lessons. There were $9$ people who attended an Ivy League school without taking piano lessons, so 

$\qquad 9 = 0.0625 \cdot \text{(total number of people)}$  

$\qquad \text{(total number of people)}=\dfrac{9}{0.0625} = 144$.

- | - | -
**Ivy League** | $\begin{align} &\text{Row %} \\ &\text{Column %} \\ &\text{Total %} \end{align}$  | $\begin{align} &\text{25} \\ &\text{8.3} \\ &\text{2.1} \end{align}$ | $\begin{align} &\text{75} \\ &\text{8.3} \\ &\pink{\text{6.25}} \end{align}$ | $\begin{align} &\text{100} \\ &\text{------} \\ &\text{8.3} \end{align}$

Now that we know there are $144$ people total, we can find the number of people who took piano lessons. The pink $25$ in the table below tells us that $25\%$ of all people in the survey took piano lessons. The number of people who took piano lessons is:

$\qquad 144\cdot 0.25 = 36$.

- | - | -

Knowledge that someone did not attend an Ivy League school does not give us any information about whether or not they took piano lessons. This also shows there is no association.

Only the following are correct:  

- No, because $91.7\%$ of people who didn't take piano lessons did not attend an Ivy League school, and $91.7\%$ of all people did not attend an Ivy League school.
- No, because $75\%$ of people who did not attend an Ivy League school did not take piano lessons, and $75\%$ of all people did not take piano lessons.

choices.content: No, because $91.7\%$ of people who did not take piano lessons did not attend an Ivy League school, but only $75\%$ of people who did not attend an Ivy League school didn't take piano lessons.
Yes, because the joint relative frequency for people who did not take piano lessons  and who did not attend an Ivy League school is the greatest.
No, because $91.7\%$ of people who didn't take piano lessons did not attend an Ivy League school, and $91.7\%$ of all people did not attend an Ivy League school.
Yes, because $25\%$ of people who attended an Ivy League school took piano lessons, but only $8.3\%$ of people who took piano lessons attended an Ivy League school.
No, because $75\%$ of people who did not attend an Ivy League school did not take piano lessons, and $75\%$ of all people did not take piano lessons.

Eddie will enter high school next year.  His older brother tells him that everybody in high school plays a musical instrument.  Skeptical, Eddie decides to investigate.  Throughout the next month, he asks a randomly selected sample of high-school students whether they play an instrument.  The table below shows what Eddie finds.  

**Use the data below to estimate the following percentages.**  
Round to the nearest *hundredth* of a percent.

 |**$9^{\text{th}}$** | $10^{\text{th}}$| $11^{\text{th}}$ | $12^{\text{th}}$| Row total
- | - | -
**Plays an instrument** |$7$ | $3$| $5$ | $9$ | $24$
**Does not play an instrument** | $14$ | $22$| $17$ | $9$| $62$
**Column total** | $21$ | $25$ | $22$ | $18$ | $86$  

Percentage of all students that play an instrument $=$ [[☃ input-number 1]] $\%$  
Percentage of all $10^{\text{th}}$ graders that play an instrument $=$ [[☃ input-number 2]] $\%$  
Percentage of all instrument-playing students that are $11^{\text{th}}$ or $12^{\text{th}}$ graders $=$ [[☃ input-number 3]] $\%$
hints:
There are $86$ total students, and $24$ of them play an instrument.  

$\qquad \dfrac{24}{86} \approx 0.2791=27.91\%$

There are $25$ students in $10^{\text{th}}$ grade, and $3$ of those $10^{\text{th}}$ graders play instruments.

$\qquad \dfrac{3}{25}=0.12=12\%$

Of the $24$ students who play instruments, $5$ of them are in $11^{\text{th}}$ grade and $9$ of them are in $12^{\text{th}}$ grade.  So there are $14$ total $11^{\text{th}}$ and $12^{\text{th}}$ graders who play instruments.

$\qquad \dfrac{14}{24} \approx 0.5833=58.33\%$

Percentage of all students that play an instrument $= 27.91\%$  
Percentage of $10^{\text{th}}$ graders that play an instrument $=12\%$  
Percentage of all instrument-playing students that are $11^{\text{th}}$ or $12^{\text{th}}$ graders$=58.33$$\%$

assessment_items.question_content:
At a popular rock-climbing site in Yosemite National Park, a randomly selected sample of $200$ climbers are asked whether they have ever had a climbing accident and whether they are certified climbers.  The results are shown below. 

Suppose you are searching for a climbing partner at Yosemite and you meet Garrick, Molly, and Ryan. Garrick just got in a bad climbing accident. Molly has a certification card attached to her harness, and you know nothing about Ryan.  

**Based on the frequency table below, calculate the following probabilities.**  
Round to the nearest *hundredth* of a percent.

|Certified | Not certified|  Row total
- | - | -
**Has had an accident** |$2$ |$6$| $8$ 
**Has never had an accident** | $138$|$54$ |$192$
**Column total** |$140$ | $60$ |$200$   

Probability that Garrick is certified $=$ [[☃ input-number 1]] $\%$  
Probability that Molly has not had an accident $=$ [[☃ input-number 2]] $\%$  
Probability that Ryan is both certified and has never had an accident $=$ [[☃ input-number 3]] $\%$
hints:
We know that Garrick has been in an accident.  There were $8$ climbers in the sample that had experienced an accident, and only $2$ of them were certified.  

$P\text{(Garrick is certified)} = \dfrac{2}{8}=0.25=25\%$

We know Molly is certified.  There were $140$ certified climbers, and $138$ of them had never experienced an accident.  

$P\text{(Molly has not experienced an accident)} = \dfrac{138}{140}\approx 0.9857 = 98.57\%$

We have no information about Ryan.  There were $200$ total climbers, and $138$ of them were both certified and had never experienced an accident.

$P\text{(Ryan is certified and has not experienced an accident)} = \dfrac{138}{200}= 0.69 = 69\%$

Probability that Garrick is certified $=$ $25\%$  
Probability that Molly has not experienced an accident $=$ $98.57\%$  
Probability that Ryan is certified and has not experienced an accident $=$ $69\%$

When a basketball player makes a trip to the free throw line, he takes two consecutive shots.  It is often wondered whether these two shots are independent or dependent: does the probability of making the second free throw *depend* on whether a player makes the first free throw?

After analyzing data for basketball star Albert Jones, statisticians determined that his first and second free throws are entirely *independent events*.  The frequency table below shows the data that analysts used to determine this independence.

hints:

$\qquad P\text{(Albert makes second shot)} = \dfrac{180}{250} = 0.72$  

Albert has a $72\%$ probability of making his second free throw.  Since these are independent events, the data should show that Albert makes his second free throw $72\%$ of the time that he *makes his first free throw*, and $72\%$ of the time he *misses his first free throw*.

Albert makes his first free throw $200$ times and misses his first free throw $50$ times.

\\
&= 144\end{align}$

Albert makes both free throws $144$ times.

Albert misses his first free throw and makes his second $36$ times.

$\qquad 50 - 36= 14$ times Albert misses both shots.
